images.caption: Figure 1:Universality of Fourier Features and Convergent Evolution. (Left) Fourier spectrum of number embeddings across three architecture families: Transformer LLMs, non-Transformer LLMs, and classical word embeddings. Each row shows the median-normalized magnitude at each Fourier frequency. All models exhibit consistent spikes at frequencies of periodT=2,5,10T=2,5,10, etc. (Right) Convergent Evolution of various models studied in this paper. It shows two types of convergence:spectral convergencewhere models learn Fourier spikes, andgeometric convergencewhere models learn modular probes.

Figure 2:A “Spiky” Fourier Spectrum Does Not Imply Good Feature Learning.(Left) Token embeddings of Transformer, Gated DeltaNet and LSTM, and even simply the Number Token Distribution Frequency exhibit distinct Fourier spikes atT=2,5T=2,5, and1010. (Middle) Linear probes reveal only the Transformer and Gated DeltaNet learned functional modular arithmetic with high Cohen’sκ\kappa, while others remain at chance. (Right)Theorem˜1explains this discrepancy through the internal noise structure of theT=10T=10embeddings.

Figure 3:Examples for the Proof ofTheorem˜1Part (ii) in §A.3. In the proof, every numbern∈{0,…,N−1}n\in\{0,\dots,N-1\}has a unique decompositionn=r+m​Tn=r+mTwith residuer=nmodT∈{0,…,T−1}r=n\bmod T\in\{0,\dots,T{-}1\}and block indexm=⌊n/T⌋∈{0,…,K−1}m=\lfloor n/T\rfloor\in\{0,\dots,K{-}1\}, whereK=N/TK=N/T.
We set the embeddinge​(n)=A​r+B​me(n)=Ar+Bm, so thatAAcontrols the between-class scatterSBS_{B}(and henceΦT=N⋅SB\Phi_{T}=N\cdot S_{B}) whileBBcontrols only the within-class scatterSWS_{W}, with neither parameter affecting the other.
As illustrated in the figure, fixingAAproduces a persistent Fourier spike at periodTTregardless ofBB: whenBBis small the residue classes cluster into linearly separable groups, but asBBgrows the embeddings interleave across classes so that the best linear classification accuracy goes closer to random guessing1/T1/T.

Figure 4:Spectral convergence is universal but geometric convergence depends on the data signal.(Left) Fourier spectra of Transformer embeddings trained under data perturbations inTable˜1.
All perturbations produce similar spikes to the original at periodsT=2,5,10T=2,5,10, includingUnigram Replace, which destroys all co-occurrence structure among number tokens.
(Right) Cohen’sκ\kappaof linear probes for mod-TTclassification tells a different story:Original,Isolate-8, andContext Length 64achieve strong probing, but with shorter context length or fewer numbers within each sequence (e.g.Isolate-2) is much weaker atT=5T=5and1010.Swap Numbersdrops substantially, andUnigram Replacefalls to chance.

Figure 5:Geometric convergence depends on architecture and optimizer.(Left) Fourier magnitude spectra of number embeddings across architectures and optimizers; all exhibit spikes at periodsT=2,5,10T=2,5,10, including theLSTMand classical word embeddings.
All three architectures produce similar Fourier spectra under both optimizers.
(Right) Mod-TTprobe accuracy separates the models into tiers.
With both Muon and AdamW,Transformer,Gated DeltaNet, andMamba-2all achieve strong geometric convergence, while theLSTMremains near zero.
Muon outperforms AdamW forTransformerandGated DeltaNet, butMamba-2with AdamW slightly outperforms its Muon counterpart.PPMIandword2vecfall in between.

Figure 6:Tokenization determines convergence in arithmetic.(Top) In 9-digit addition, both Muon and AdamW converge to the same spectral structure with sharp Fourier peaks and near-perfectκ\kappafor mod 2, 5, and 10, showing both spectral and geometric convergence.
(Bottom) In 3-digit addition, where every operand and sum fits in a single token, the Fourier spectra vary across optimizers and random seeds, andκ\kapparemains near chance for all moduli.
Multi-token tokenization forces modular subproblems that produce convergent representations; single-token tokenization leaves the representation unconstrained.

Figure 7:Emergence of Fourier structure in a constructed embeddinge​(n)=A​(nmodT)+B​⌊n/T⌋e(n)=A(n\bmod T)+B\lfloor n/T\rfloorwithT=10T=10,A=5A=5,N=1000N=1000. Each dot is a numbernnplaced at its scalar embedding value on the horizontal axis and colored by its classnmodTn\bmod T; sincee​(n)e(n)is one-dimensional, the vertical coordinate carries no information and is jittered purely for visibility so that overlapping points of different classes remain distinguishable.Top (B=0.03B=0.03): within-block drift is small relative to between-class spacing, so the sorted line separates into 10 clean color bands: a linear classifier one​(n)e(n)recovers the mod-10 residue at near-perfect accuracy, and the DFT concentrates energy at the fundamental frequencyν=1/T=0.1\nu=1/T=0.1.Bottom (B=21B=21): the between-block driftBBdominates, interleaving classes along the line so that every mod-10 lane spans the full range; best linear accuracy collapses to≈1/T+ε\approx 1/T+\varepsilonwithε=0.9\varepsilon=0.9%. The Fourier spectrum keeps the same peak atν=0.1\nu=0.1(sameΦT\Phi_{T}), showing that periodic energy at the fundamental is a property of the construction itself, independent of whether class identity is linearly decodable.

Figure 8:Ablation on the depth of LSTM models.We find that reducing the number of layers to 4 (green) instead of 12 (blue) does not change the phenomenon: both will learn fourier spikes but no probing performance.

Figure 9:Spectral and geometric convergence co-emerge gradually during pretraining.Fourier powerΦT\Phi_{T}(left axis) and linear probe accuracy (right axis) for a 300M Transformer trained with Muon, shown forT=2,5,10T=2,5,10.
Both metrics increase smoothly throughout training with no phase transition, in contrast to the sudden emergence observed in grokking on modular arithmetic tasks inNandaet al.(2023).

Figure 10:Training dynamics for Transformers trained on addition (two seeds).(Left)In 9-digit addition, both Muon and AdamW converge smoothly to near-perfect train and test accuracy, with no grokking phase.(Right)In 3-digit addition, under seed 42, training accuracy reaches 100% for both optimizers, but generalization is optimizer- and seed-dependent. AdamW exhibits a quick grokking under seed 42 (test accuracy jumps around 1.5–2B tokens) but not under seed 123 (where training accuracy can’t reach 100% and test accuracy remains random). This confirms that single-token addition imposes no consistent pressure toward structured representations.
Figure 11:Structural Attribution Results with RFM probes.
Figure 12:Structural Attribution Results with 2-layer MLP probes.

Figure 13:Circular probe projections of token embeddings onto the unit circle for mod-10
classification. Each point is a number tokenn∈[0,999]n\in[0,999]projected to 2-D by the probe
and normalized; color indicatesnmod10n\bmod 10. (Left) embeddings from a model trained
on 9-digit addition cluster sharply by residue class (84%84\%test accuracy), indicating that
the token embedding layer has learned a geometrically organised, clock-like representation.
(Right) embeddings from a model trained on 3-digit addition show no angular
structure (11%11\%test accuracy, near chance), consistent with the absence of Fourier peaks
in Figure6.
